mnemonics.title: HDLC Frame Types
mnemonics.content: I CAN SING U - Information (I), CAN (Control), Supervisory (S), Information (I), N-frames (Numbered), General (U-frames)

acronyms.usage: Error detection field in frame trailer, typically contains CRC value
A bit-oriented synchronous data link layer protocol used for framing
A data link layer protocol for direct connections between two nodes
IBM's precursor to HDLC, used for synchronous serial communication
Mathematical algorithm used in FCS for error detection in frames
Special character used in character stuffing to escape data sequences

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a conveyor belt: data bits flow through a 'stuffer' machine that watches for five '1' bananas in a row and automatically adds a '0' apple after them. At the receiving end, a 'destuffer' removes these apples.
Think of a frame as an envelope: header = address/return address, payload = letter inside, trailer = seal/postmark for verification (FCS)
Picture a start/end flag as red ribbon on a gift box - it marks where the box (frame) begins and ends, making it easy to identify the package.

analogies.analogy: Framing is like pagination in a book - each page (frame) has a number (header), content (payload), and chapter ending marker (trailer) to help readers identify where each section starts and ends.
Bit stuffing is like adding a pause in speech to prevent confusion - if someone says 'well well well' (six 1s), you insert 'um' (0) to break the pattern so it doesn't sound like 'well well' repeated.
Character stuffing is like using quotes in writing - if you want to include a quote mark inside your text, you put a backslash before it to show it's part of the data, not a delimiter.
